nativeRange.habitat: Silty coastal waters, sheltered bays, and reef-adjacent lagoons at depths of roughly 2–15 m, where adults are typically associated with a host sea anemone

decorPreferences: scattered rockwork forming a defensible territory

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of frozen and live meaty foods (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped seafood) alongside herbivore preparations rich in spirulina and marine algae. Has been described as an aggressive eater that readily takes prepared foods.

avoidWith: additional Amphiprion ephippium individuals (unless an established mated pair)
ornamental shrimp once the clownfish is established

summary: The Red Saddle Clownfish (Amphiprion ephippium) is a deep red-orange anemonefish from the Eastern Indian Ocean, generally considered intermediate in care. It tends to be semi-aggressive toward conspecifics and benefits from a host anemone.
careNotes:
The Red Saddle Clownfish, also widely sold as the Red Saddleback Clownfish or Fire Clownfish, is one of the more striking Amphiprion species: adults are typically a uniform deep red-orange with a single dark 'saddle' patch on the upper flank and no head bar in mature fish. Juveniles often show a thin white head line that gradually fades as the dark saddle emerges with maturity. This distinguishes A. ephippium from the superficially similar A. polymnus (Black Saddleback Clownfish), which retains a prominent white head bar throughout life.

Like all clownfish, A. ephippium is protandrous: every individual is born male, and the dominant individual in a pair becomes the female (the larger fish). When establishing a pair, introducing both fish simultaneously or buying a confirmed mated pair is generally advised to reduce aggression. This species has been known to be more aggressive toward conspecifics and other clownfish than Ocellaris or Percula — best kept singly or as a true pair, never with other clownfish species in the same tank.

Native host anemones reported in the wild and accepted in aquaria include the Bubble-Tip Anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) and the Sebae / Leathery Sea Anemone (Heteractis crispa). A host anemone is not strictly required for captive survival, though its presence has been reported to reduce stress and encourage natural behavior; in the absence of an anemone, the fish has been known to attempt to host on stony coral polyps, which the corals do not tolerate well.

Wild habitat tends toward silty coastal waters and sheltered bays rather than pristine outer reefs, which may make the species somewhat more tolerant of moderate nutrient levels than strict SPS-reef inhabitants.
breedingNotes: Amphiprion ephippium has been reported to breed in home aquariums, with husbandry roughly comparable to other Amphiprion species. The female (the larger of the pair) lays demersal eggs on a flat, cleaned surface near the host anemone or chosen site; both parents participate in guarding and aerating the clutch, with the male performing the bulk of fanning duties. Eggs have been reported to hatch in approximately 8–11 days depending on water temperature. Larval rearing is the primary challenge: newly hatched fry require dedicated rearing tanks and an initial diet of rotifers, transitioning to baby brine shrimp as they develop. Captive-bred specimens are increasingly available and tend to be hardier and more peaceful than wild-caught individuals.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs have been reported to spawn on flat surfaces near a host anemone in captivity; larval rearing is considered more demanding than for Ocellaris or Percula clownfish, requiring dedicated rearing tanks and rotifers as first food.
